title: Cat Bleeding After Heat Cycle: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Persistent bleeding in cats after heat is a high-priority emergency. Learn about the risks of Pyometra and why immediate vet care is required.
content_html:
TL;DR: Visible bleeding or discharge after a cat's heat cycle is never normal and often indicates a life-threatening uterine infection called pyometra. You should seek immediate emergency veterinary care to prevent fatal complications like sepsis or uterine rupture.

What is persistent post-heat bleeding in cats and why is it concerning?

Unlike dogs or humans, it is not normal for a cat to have visible bleeding during or after their heat cycle (estrus). While a very slight pinkish tint might occasionally be seen in some cats, persistent bright red blood or thick, colored discharge is a sign of a serious medical issue within the reproductive tract.

Is my cat having a veterinary emergency if she is bleeding after her heat cycle?

Urgency Level: High. Bleeding after a heat cycle is a classic clinical sign of Pyometra, a life-threatening bacterial infection of the uterus. This condition is critical because:

  • The cervix begins to close after the heat cycle ends, which can trap bacteria inside the reproductive tract.
  • Trapped bacteria lead to a massive infection and accumulation of fluid.
  • If left untreated, this can cause the uterus to rupture or lead to fatal sepsis.
  • You should seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

How can taking a photo of my cat's discharge help the veterinary triage team?

If you can safely do so, taking a clear photograph of the discharge on your cat or on the surfaces where she has been sitting can be extremely helpful for the following reasons:

  • It allows the veterinary triage team to assess the color, consistency, and volume of the blood or fluid.
  • It helps them determine if the discharge is hemorrhagic (fresh blood) or purulent (pus-filled).
  • Providing visual evidence can speed up the diagnostic process and treatment plan upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A hemorrhagic vulvar discharge in a cat after a heat cycle warrants emergency triage. Potential differentials include metritis, vaginitis, pyometra (especially if accompanied by lethargy, anorexia, polyuria/polydipsia, vomiting, uterine enlargement, and/or shock), and other causes of post-partum hemorrhage. Physical examination should assess for fever, dehydration, uterine enlargement, and the character of any vaginal discharge. Diagnostic evaluation should include a complete blood count to evaluate for leukocytosis/leukopenia and anemia.

Chapter: Reproduction

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1399)

faqs.question: Is it ever normal for a cat to bleed from her vulva?
faqs.answer: No. Any visible blood from a cat's reproductive tract is considered abnormal and should be evaluated by a veterinarian immediately.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of a uterine infection (Pyometra)?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include lethargy, increased thirst, frequent urination, loss of appetite, and sometimes a swollen or painful abdomen.

faqs.question: Can I wait until my regular vet opens tomorrow?
faqs.answer: No. Because conditions like Pyometra can cause a cat to decline rapidly, persistent bleeding should be treated as an emergency that requires immediate consultation with an after-hours clinic.
